seoTitle: How to Create a Dashboard With Filters in Jira
metaDescription: Build a Jira dashboard powered by saved filters so you can monitor exactly the issues and metrics that matter to your team.
intro: A generic dashboard shows too much or too little unless it's built around filters tailored to your project. This guide covers creating a dashboard in Jira and wiring gadgets to saved filters so the data stays relevant.

steps.heading: Create your saved filters first
steps.body: Build and save the issue searches you want to track, such as open bugs or issues due this week, before setting up the dashboard.

steps.heading: Start a new dashboard
steps.body: Go to the Dashboards area and create a new dashboard, giving it a clear name and description.

steps.heading: Add filter-based gadgets
steps.body: Add gadgets like Filter Results or a pie chart, then point each one at the saved filter you created earlier.

steps.heading: Arrange the layout
steps.body: Rearrange gadgets on the dashboard so the most important information is visible without scrolling.

steps.heading: Share the dashboard with your team
steps.body: Set sharing permissions so the right people can view or edit the dashboard going forward.

faq.question: Do I need to create filters before building the dashboard?
faq.answer: It helps, since most dashboard gadgets rely on a saved filter as their data source.

faq.question: Can multiple people edit the same dashboard?
faq.answer: Yes, if sharing permissions are set to allow editing, multiple team members can update the dashboard's gadgets.

faq.question: How many gadgets can I add to one dashboard?
faq.answer: There's no strict limit, though dashboards with too many gadgets can become slow to load and hard to read.

faq.question: Can I filter a dashboard by sprint or epic?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as your underlying saved filter includes that criteria, the dashboard gadget will reflect it.
